_instructions.purpose: Template for one UPSC Prelims GS question. Feed this + paper briefing to the content factory to generate 100 questions per paper.
_instructions.subject_split: Polity 18 | History 16 | Geography 14 | Economy 14 | Environment 14 | SciTech 10 | CurrentAffairs 14 — UPSC Prelims weightage, tune per paper.
_instructions.scoring: From APP.exam.prelimsGS — +2 correct | -0.66 wrong | 0 blank | Max 200. NEVER hardcode in content.

formats.statements_count: 2-4 statements in the statements array; options are counts ('Only one', 'Only two', ...) or combinations ('1 and 2 only').
formats.match_pairs: statements array holds 'List-I : List-II' rows; options are combination counts.
formats.assertion_reason: statements[0] = Assertion, statements[1] = Reason; options are the standard four A/R verdicts.

field_rules.question_id: Follow id_format. MUST be unique across all papers.
field_rules.subject: Polity | History | Geography | Economy | Environment | SciTech | CurrentAffairs (exact string)
field_rules.topic: Broad topic — e.g. 'Fundamental Rights', 'Modern History', 'Monetary Policy'
field_rules.subtopic: Specific sub-type — e.g. 'Article 21 jurisprudence', 'Market reforms of Khaljis'
field_rules.statements: Required for non-single formats. Omit for single.
field_rules.difficulty: Easy | Medium | Hard. Target mix: ~30% Easy, 45% Medium, 25% Hard
field_rules.expected_time_sec: Realistic seconds for an aspirant near the cutoff. Easy=40-60s, Medium=60-90s, Hard=80-120s
field_rules.text: The question stem. For statement formats, the lead-in line ('Consider the following statements:').
field_rules.options: Keys A B C D. All plausible — UPSC distractors are designed, not filler.
field_rules.answer: Single letter: A | B | C | D. NEVER sent to the client.
field_rules.explanation: 2-4 sentences. WHY correct AND why the most tempting wrong option fails.
field_rules.elimination_path: THE UPSC DNA FIELD. Narrate how a smart guesser gets this right WITHOUT full knowledge — extreme-word elimination ('only', 'all', 'never'), pair logic in match questions, statement-couple logic. If genuinely not eliminable, write 'NONE' — analytics skip it.

quality_rules: UPSC Prelims GS level — conceptual, multi-statement, current-linked. Not state-PSC recall.
All 4 options plausible; the trap option must be the one a half-prepared aspirant picks.
elimination_path is mandatory thinking: at least ~40% of a paper should be solvable by technique.
Questions must be original — paraphrase PYQ patterns, never lift verbatim.
No question repeats a concept already covered earlier in the same paper.

_example_completed.subject: Polity
statements: The right to life under Article 21 is available to foreigners in India.
The freedoms under Article 19 are available only to citizens.
The right to property is a fundamental right under Part III.
_example_completed.text: Consider the following statements: How many of the above statements are correct?
_example_completed.explanation: Article 21 protects 'persons', so it extends to foreigners; Article 19 is expressly limited to citizens. The right to property was removed from Part III by the 44th Amendment (1978) and survives only as a constitutional right under Article 300A — so statement 3 is wrong.
_example_completed.elimination_path: If you remember only the 44th Amendment headline — property is no longer a fundamental right — statement 3 dies instantly, which kills option C. Between 'Only one' and 'Only two', the 'persons vs citizens' wording of Articles 21 and 19 is the classic statement-pair UPSC tests; both are standard, so 'Only two'.
